name: Infective Endocarditis
signs:
Fever
New or changing heart murmur
Janeway lesions (painless palmar/plantar macules)
Osler nodes (painful fingertip nodules)
Splinter hemorrhages
Petechiae
Splenomegaly
Embolic phenomena (stroke, renal infarct)
treatment:
1. Blood cultures x3 from separate sites before antibiotics
2. Empiric IV antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftriaxone)
3. Tailor to culture results - typically 4-6 weeks IV
4. Surgical valve replacement if: heart failure, uncontrolled infection, large vegetations >10mm, embolic events
meds:
Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h
Ceftriaxone 2g IV q24h
Nafcillin 2g IV q4h (MSSA)
Gentamicin 1mg/kg IV q8h (synergy)
Daptomycin 6-8mg/kg IV daily (alternative)
ddx: Rheumatic fever, SLE (Libman-Sacks), Atrial myxoma, Bacteremia without endocarditis, Vasculitis
labs:
Blood cultures x3 (before antibiotics)
CBC (anemia, leukocytosis)
ESR/CRP elevated
Urinalysis (hematuria)
TTE then TEE if high suspicion
Rheumatoid factor (may be positive)
notes:
Modified Duke criteria: 2 major, 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor
Major: positive blood cultures (typical organisms), endocardial involvement on echo
Most common: S. aureus (acute), Viridans streptococci (subacute)
Prophylaxis: amoxicillin 2g PO 1h before dental procedures in high-risk patients
description: An infection of the inner lining of the heart chambers and valves, usually caused by bacteria entering the bloodstream and colonizing damaged heart valves.

name: Pericarditis
signs:
Sharp pleuritic chest pain
Pain improves sitting up and leaning forward
Pericardial friction rub (scratchy, 3-component)
Fever
Dyspnea
Diffuse ST elevation on ECG
PR depression
treatment:
1. NSAIDs (ibuprofen or aspirin) as first-line
2. Colchicine to reduce recurrence
3. Restrict activity until symptoms resolve and CRP normalizes
4. Corticosteroids only if contraindication to NSAIDs/colchicine
5. Pericardiocentesis if tamponade develops
meds:
Ibuprofen 600mg PO TID x1-2 weeks then taper
Colchicine 0.5mg BID x3 months (weight <70kg: 0.5mg daily)
Aspirin 750-1000mg PO TID (if post-MI)
Preднисolone 0.25-0.5mg/kg/day (refractory cases)
ddx: Acute MI, Myocarditis, PE, Aortic dissection, Costochondritis, Pleuritis, GERD
labs:
ECG (diffuse ST elevation, PR depression)
Troponin (may elevate = myopericarditis)
CRP/ESR
CBC
Echo (pericardial effusion?)
CXR (enlarged cardiac silhouette if large effusion)
notes:
Viral most common cause (Coxsackie, Echo, adenovirus)
ECG stages: 1) diffuse ST elevation + PR depression, 2) normalization, 3) diffuse T wave inversion, 4) normalization
Recurrence in 15-30% - colchicine reduces this
Avoid anticoagulation (risk of hemorrhagic pericarditis)
description: Inflammation of the pericardium, the thin sac surrounding the heart, most commonly caused by viral infections and resulting in sharp chest pain.

name: Aortic Stenosis
signs:
Crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at RUSB
Radiates to carotids
Syncope with exertion
Angina
Dyspnea on exertion
Pulsus parvus et tardus
Narrow pulse pressure
S4 gallop
treatment:
1. Surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR) for symptomatic severe AS
2. Transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) if high surgical risk
3. Medical management: cautious diuretics for fluid overload
4. Avoid vasodilators and vigorous exercise
5. Serial echo monitoring if asymptomatic
meds:
Furosemide 20-40mg PO daily (cautious volume management)
Avoid ACE inhibitors in severe symptomatic AS
Statins (may slow progression - debated)
Digoxin if concurrent AFib
Endocarditis prophylaxis for prosthetic valves
ddx: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, Aortic sclerosis, Mitral regurgitation, Subaortic stenosis, Supravalvular AS
labs:
TTE with Doppler (valve area, gradient, EF)
ECG (LVH, LAE)
CXR (calcified aortic valve, cardiomegaly)
BNP
Cardiac catheterization if discordant data
CT calcium scoring
notes:
Classic triad: syncope, angina, heart failure (SAD)
Severe AS: valve area <1.0 cm2, mean gradient >40mmHg, peak velocity >4m/s
Average survival after symptoms: angina 5yr, syncope 3yr, HF 2yr
Bicuspid aortic valve is most common cause in patients <70
description: Narrowing of the aortic valve opening that restricts blood flow from the heart to the body, often due to age-related calcification or a congenital bicuspid valve.

name: Mitral Regurgitation
signs:
Holosystolic murmur at apex
Radiates to axilla
Dyspnea on exertion
Orthopnea
Fatigue
Palpitations
S3 gallop
Laterally displaced PMI
treatment:
1. Medical management for chronic compensated MR
2. Surgery (repair preferred over replacement) for severe symptomatic MR
3. Surgery if asymptomatic with EF 30-60% or LVESD >=40mm
4. MitraClip for high surgical risk patients
5. Afterload reduction and diuretics
meds:
ACE inhibitors (lisinopril 5-40mg daily)
Diuretics (furosemide 20-80mg daily)
Beta-blockers if AFib
Anticoagulation if AFib (apixaban 5mg BID)
Hydralazine if ACEi intolerant
ddx: Aortic stenosis, VSD, Tricuspid regurgitation, Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, MVP
labs:
TTE with Doppler (severity grading, EF, LA size)
TEE (detailed valve anatomy pre-surgery)
ECG (LAE, AFib, LVH)
CXR (cardiomegaly, pulmonary congestion)
BNP
Cardiac MRI if echo inconclusive
notes:
Primary (degenerative, endocarditis, rheumatic) vs Secondary (ischemic, dilated CM)
Severe MR: regurgitant volume >=60mL, EROA >=0.4cm2, vena contracta >=0.7cm
Acute MR (chordal rupture, papillary muscle rupture): surgical emergency
Repair has better outcomes than replacement - refer to experienced center
description: A condition where the mitral valve doesn't close tightly, allowing blood to flow backward into the left atrium during each heartbeat.

name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy
signs:
Dyspnea on exertion
Orthopnea
Fatigue
Peripheral edema
JVD
S3 gallop
Laterally displaced PMI
Mitral/tricuspid regurgitation murmurs
Hepatomegaly
treatment:
1. Standard GDMT for HFrEF: ACEi/ARB/ARNI + BB + MRA + SGLT2i
2. Diuretics for volume overload
3. ICD if EF <=35% despite 3 months GDMT
4. CRT if LBBB + QRS >=150ms + EF <=35%
5. Treat underlying cause if identified
6. LVAD or transplant evaluation for end-stage
meds:
Sacubitril/valsartan 24/26mg BID titrate to 97/103mg BID
Carvedilol 3.125mg BID titrate to 25mg BID
Spironolactone 25mg daily
Dapagliflozin 10mg daily
Furosemide 20-160mg daily
Ivabradine 5-7.5mg BID if HR >70
ddx: Ischemic cardiomyopathy, Myocarditis, Valvular heart disease, Hypertensive cardiomyopathy, Peripartum cardiomyopathy, Alcoholic cardiomyopathy
labs:
TTE (dilated LV, reduced EF, global hypokinesis)
BNP/NT-proBNP
CBC, BMP, LFTs, TSH
Iron studies (hemochromatosis)
HIV, hepatitis panel
Cardiac MRI (fibrosis pattern)
Genetic testing if familial
notes:
Most common cause of heart transplantation
Idiopathic in ~50%, familial ~25%, alcohol, chemotherapy (doxorubicin), viral
Reversible causes: tachycardia-mediated, alcohol, thyroid, peripartum
Genetic counseling and screening of first-degree relatives recommended
description: A disease of the heart muscle where the left ventricle becomes enlarged and weakened, reducing the heart's ability to pump blood effectively.

name: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
signs:
Systolic murmur that increases with Valsalva/standing
Dyspnea on exertion
Syncope or presyncope with exertion
Chest pain
Palpitations
Bisferiens pulse
S4 gallop
Double apical impulse
treatment:
1. Avoid dehydration, strenuous exercise, and Valsalva
2. Beta-blockers first-line for symptoms
3. Verapamil or diltiazem if BB intolerant
4. Disopyramide for refractory obstruction
5. Septal myectomy (surgical) or alcohol septal ablation
6. ICD for high-risk SCD patients
7. Mavacamten for obstructive HCM
meds:
Metoprolol 50-200mg daily
Verapamil 120-480mg daily (avoid with BB)
Disopyramide 100-200mg TID
Mavacamten 5-15mg daily
Avoid: digoxin, diuretics, vasodilators, ACEi in obstructive HCM
ddx: Athlete's heart, Hypertensive LVH, Aortic stenosis, Fabry disease, Amyloidosis, Friedreich ataxia
labs:
TTE (asymmetric septal hypertrophy >=15mm, SAM of mitral valve, LVOT gradient)
ECG (LVH, deep Q waves, T wave inversions)
Cardiac MRI (late gadolinium enhancement)
Holter monitor (arrhythmias)
Genetic testing (sarcomere mutations)
Exercise stress test
notes:
Most common cause of SCD in young athletes
Autosomal dominant - screen first-degree relatives
SCD risk factors: family hx SCD, syncope, NSVT, massive LVH >=30mm, abnormal BP response to exercise
Avoid competitive sports if diagnosed
SAM = systolic anterior motion of mitral valve
description: A genetic condition causing abnormal thickening of the heart muscle, which can obstruct blood flow and increase the risk of sudden cardiac death, especially in young athletes.

name: Ventricular Tachycardia
signs:
Palpitations
Dizziness/lightheadedness
Syncope
Chest pain
Dyspnea
Hemodynamic instability
Wide complex tachycardia on ECG
AV dissociation
Capture/fusion beats
treatment:
1. Pulseless VT: defibrillation per ACLS protocol
2. Unstable with pulse: synchronized cardioversion
3. Stable monomorphic VT: IV amiodarone or procainamide
4. Polymorphic VT (normal QT): treat as VF, correct ischemia
5. Torsades de pointes: IV magnesium, overdrive pacing
6. Long-term: ICD implantation, catheter ablation
meds:
Amiodarone 150mg IV over 10min, then 1mg/min x6h, then 0.5mg/min
Procainamide 20-50mg/min IV (max 17mg/kg)
Lidocaine 1-1.5mg/kg IV bolus
Magnesium 2g IV over 5min (Torsades)
Sotalol 80-160mg PO BID (prevention)
Mexiletine 200mg PO TID
ddx: SVT with aberrancy, WPW with AFib, Hyperkalemia, Accelerated idioventricular rhythm, Artifact
labs:
12-lead ECG (wide QRS >=120ms, rate >100)
Continuous monitoring
BMP (K+, Mg2+, Ca2+)
Troponin (ischemic trigger)
TTE (structural heart disease)
Drug levels if applicable
Cardiac MRI/catheterization
notes:
Brugada criteria to distinguish VT from SVT with aberrancy
AV dissociation is pathognomonic for VT
VT in structural heart disease: high risk for SCD, needs ICD
Idiopathic VT (no structural heart disease): better prognosis, consider ablation
Sustained = >30 seconds or hemodynamic compromise
description: A fast, abnormal heart rhythm originating in the ventricles, which can be life-threatening if sustained and may degenerate into cardiac arrest.

name: Supraventricular Tachycardia
signs:
Sudden onset palpitations
Rapid regular heart rate (150-250 bpm)
Dizziness
Chest tightness
Dyspnea
Anxiety
Neck pulsations
Polyuria (ANP release)
Abrupt termination
treatment:
1. Vagal maneuvers (Valsalva, carotid sinus massage, ice water to face)
2. Adenosine 6mg rapid IV push, then 12mg if no response
3. IV diltiazem or verapamil if adenosine fails
4. Synchronized cardioversion if hemodynamically unstable
5. Catheter ablation for recurrent SVT (curative)
meds:
Adenosine 6mg IV rapid push, repeat 12mg x2
Diltiazem 0.25mg/kg IV over 2min
Verapamil 5-10mg IV over 2min
Metoprolol 5mg IV q5min x3
Flecainide 50-150mg PO BID (prevention, no structural HD)
Diltiazem 120-360mg PO daily (prevention)
ddx: Sinus tachycardia, Atrial flutter, Atrial fibrillation, VT, Anxiety/panic attack
labs:
12-lead ECG during episode (narrow QRS, regular, P waves absent or retrograde)
ECG after conversion (look for delta wave = WPW, short PR)
BMP
TSH
Holter or event monitor
EP study if recurrent
notes:
AVNRT (60%): most common type, dual AV nodal pathways
AVRT (30%): accessory pathway (WPW if manifest)
Atrial tachycardia (10%): P waves visible, may be automatic
AVOID AV nodal blockers in WPW with AFib (can cause VF)
Pill-in-pocket: flecainide 200-300mg PO for infrequent episodes
description: A rapid heart rhythm originating above the ventricles, causing episodes of sudden fast heartbeat that usually start and stop abruptly.

name: Complete Heart Block
signs:
Bradycardia (20-40 bpm)
Syncope (Stokes-Adams attacks)
Dizziness
Fatigue
Dyspnea
Hypotension
Cannon A waves in JVP
Variable intensity S1
treatment:
1. Identify and treat reversible causes (MI, drugs, infection)
2. Atropine 0.5-1mg IV for symptomatic bradycardia
3. Transcutaneous pacing if unstable
4. Transvenous temporary pacing as bridge
5. Permanent pacemaker implantation (definitive)
6. Isoproterenol infusion as temporizing measure
meds:
Atropine 0.5-1mg IV q3-5min (max 3mg) - often ineffective
Isoproterenol 2-10mcg/min IV
Dopamine 5-20mcg/kg/min IV
Epinephrine 2-10mcg/min IV
Glucocorticoids if inflammatory etiology
ddx: Second-degree AV block (Mobitz II), High vagal tone, Drug effect (BB, CCB, digoxin), Inferior MI, Lyme disease, Infiltrative disease
labs:
12-lead ECG (P waves and QRS complexes independent, AV dissociation)
Continuous telemetry
BMP (electrolytes)
TSH
Lyme titers if endemic area
Troponin (MI-related)
Digoxin level if applicable
notes:
No relationship between P waves and QRS complexes
Escape rhythm: junctional 40-60bpm (narrow QRS), ventricular 20-40bpm (wide QRS)
Reversible causes: inferior MI (usually temporary), Lyme disease, drugs
All symptomatic complete heart block needs permanent pacemaker
Congenital CHB: may be well-tolerated in young patients
description: A condition where electrical signals from the heart's upper chambers completely fail to reach the lower chambers, resulting in a dangerously slow heart rate.

name: Myocarditis
signs:
Chest pain
Dyspnea
Fever
Fatigue
Palpitations
Recent viral illness
New heart failure symptoms
Arrhythmias
Cardiogenic shock (fulminant)
treatment:
1. Supportive care and hemodynamic support
2. Standard HF therapy if reduced EF (ACEi, BB, diuretics)
3. Restrict activity for 3-6 months
4. Immunosuppression for giant cell myocarditis or autoimmune
5. Mechanical support (IABP, ECMO) for fulminant cases
6. Transplant evaluation if no recovery
meds:
Lisinopril 2.5-20mg daily
Carvedilol 3.125-25mg BID
Furosemide 20-80mg IV/PO as needed
Milrinone 0.375-0.75mcg/kg/min (cardiogenic shock)
IVIG (debated, may help in viral)
Prednisone + azathioprine (giant cell myocarditis)
ddx: Acute MI, Takotsubo cardiomyopathy, PE, Pericarditis, Dilated cardiomyopathy, Sepsis-related cardiomyopathy
labs:
Troponin elevated
BNP/NT-proBNP elevated
ECG (ST changes, arrhythmias, low voltage)
Echo (wall motion abnormalities, reduced EF)
Cardiac MRI (edema, late gadolinium enhancement - Lake Louise criteria)
Endomyocardial biopsy (gold standard but rarely done)
Viral serologies
notes:
Most common cause: viral (Coxsackie B, parvovirus B19, HHV-6, SARS-CoV-2)
Giant cell myocarditis: rapidly progressive, high mortality without immunosuppression
Fulminant myocarditis: paradoxically better prognosis if survives acute phase
No competitive sports for 3-6 months after diagnosis
Cardiac MRI is best noninvasive diagnostic tool
description: Inflammation of the heart muscle, usually triggered by a viral infection, which can weaken the heart and cause arrhythmias.

name: Peripheral Arterial Disease
signs:
Intermittent claudication (leg pain with walking)
Rest pain in advanced disease
Non-healing wounds or ulcers
Diminished or absent pulses
Hair loss on extremities
Pale/cool skin
ABI <0.9
treatment:
1. Smoking cessation (most important)
2. Supervised exercise program (30-45min, 3x/week)
3. Antiplatelet therapy
4. Statin therapy
5. Cilostazol for claudication symptoms
6. Revascularization (endovascular or surgical) for critical limb ischemia or lifestyle-limiting claudication
meds:
Aspirin 81mg daily or Clopidogrel 75mg daily
Atorvastatin 40-80mg daily
Cilostazol 100mg BID (avoid in HF)
Ramipril 2.5-10mg daily
Rivaroxaban 2.5mg BID + aspirin (COMPASS trial)
ddx: Lumbar spinal stenosis (neurogenic claudication), DVT, Chronic venous insufficiency, Peripheral neuropathy, Buerger disease
labs:
Ankle-brachial index (ABI <0.9 diagnostic)
Segmental pressures
Duplex ultrasound
CTA or MRA (pre-intervention)
Lipid panel, HbA1c, BMP
Toe pressures if calcified vessels (ABI falsely elevated in diabetes)
notes:
ABI: <0.9 = PAD, <0.4 = severe/critical
Fontaine classification: I asymptomatic, II claudication, III rest pain, IV tissue loss
PAD patients have high cardiovascular mortality - screen for CAD
Rutherford classification used for surgical planning
Critical limb ischemia: rest pain >2 weeks, ulcers, or gangrene
description: Narrowing of arteries in the legs due to atherosclerosis, reducing blood flow and causing pain with walking that can progress to tissue loss.

name: Deep Vein Thrombosis
signs:
Unilateral leg swelling
Pain and tenderness along deep veins
Warmth
Erythema
Pitting edema
Homan sign (unreliable)
Dilated superficial veins
May be asymptomatic
treatment:
1. Anticoagulation is mainstay of treatment
2. DOAC preferred for most patients (rivaroxaban or apixaban)
3. Heparin bridge to warfarin if DOAC not appropriate
4. Catheter-directed thrombolysis for massive iliofemoral DVT
5. IVC filter if anticoagulation contraindicated
6. Compression stockings
7. Early ambulation
meds:
Rivaroxaban 15mg BID x21 days then 20mg daily
Apixaban 10mg BID x7 days then 5mg BID
Enoxaparin 1mg/kg SC BID (bridge)
Heparin 80u/kg IV bolus then 18u/kg/hr
Warfarin 5mg daily (target INR 2-3)
ddx: Cellulitis, Baker cyst rupture, Muscle strain/tear, Lymphedema, Superficial thrombophlebitis, Chronic venous insufficiency, Post-thrombotic syndrome
labs:
D-dimer (sensitive, not specific - use to rule out if low pretest probability)
Compression ultrasound (first-line imaging)
CT venography (if US inconclusive)
Wells score for pretest probability
Thrombophilia workup (if unprovoked, young, recurrent)
CBC, BMP, PT/INR if starting warfarin
notes:
Wells score: >=2 = DVT likely, proceed to US; <2 = check D-dimer first
Provoked (surgery, trauma, immobilization): 3 months anticoagulation
Unprovoked: consider extended anticoagulation (risk-benefit discussion)
Upper extremity DVT: consider thoracic outlet syndrome, PICC-related
Post-thrombotic syndrome: chronic swelling, pain, skin changes
description: A blood clot that forms in a deep vein, usually in the leg, which can be dangerous if it breaks loose and travels to the lungs (pulmonary embolism).

name: Stable Angina
signs:
Substernal chest pressure/tightness with exertion
Predictable onset with consistent activity level
Relieves with rest or nitroglycerin within 5 minutes
May radiate to arm, jaw, back
Dyspnea
No pain at rest
treatment:
1. Lifestyle modification: exercise, diet, smoking cessation
2. Sublingual nitroglycerin PRN for acute episodes
3. Anti-anginal therapy: beta-blocker first-line
4. Aspirin and statin for all patients
5. Stress testing for risk stratification
6. Coronary angiography if high-risk features
7. PCI or CABG for refractory symptoms or high-risk anatomy
meds:
Aspirin 81mg daily
Atorvastatin 40-80mg daily
Metoprolol succinate 25-200mg daily
Amlodipine 5-10mg daily
Nitroglycerin 0.4mg SL PRN (up to 3 doses q5min)
Isosorbide mononitrate 30-120mg daily
Ranolazine 500-1000mg BID (add-on)
ddx: Unstable angina, GERD, Costochondritis, Esophageal spasm, Aortic stenosis, Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, Anxiety
labs:
Resting ECG (may be normal)
Stress testing (exercise or pharmacologic)
Echo (LV function, wall motion)
Lipid panel
HbA1c
BMP
Coronary CTA or angiography
notes:
CCS classification: I - ordinary activity no symptoms, II - slight limitation, III - marked limitation, IV - any activity/rest
Beta-blockers reduce mortality post-MI
Nitrate-free interval of 10-14h to prevent tolerance
Modify risk factors: HTN, DM, hyperlipidemia, smoking, obesity
ICHT trial: medical therapy noninferior to PCI for stable angina
description: Predictable chest discomfort caused by reduced blood flow to the heart during physical exertion or stress, due to narrowed coronary arteries.

name: Wolff-Parkinson-White Syndrome
signs:
Palpitations
Dizziness
Syncope
Dyspnea
Chest discomfort
Sudden cardiac death (rare)
Delta wave on resting ECG
Short PR interval
treatment:
1. Acute orthodromic AVRT: vagal maneuvers, adenosine
2. Acute antidromic AVRT: procainamide or ibutilide
3. WPW + AFib: procainamide or cardioversion (AVOID AV nodal blockers)
4. Catheter ablation of accessory pathway (curative, first-line for recurrent)
5. Observation for asymptomatic patients (risk stratification)
meds:
Adenosine 6-12mg IV (orthodromic AVRT only)
Procainamide 20-50mg/min IV
Ibutilide 1mg IV over 10min
Flecainide 200-300mg PO (pill-in-pocket)
AVOID: digoxin, verapamil, diltiazem, beta-blockers in AFib+WPW
ddx: SVT (AVNRT), Atrial flutter, Ventricular tachycardia, LGL syndrome, Mahaim fiber tachycardia
labs:
12-lead ECG (short PR <120ms, delta wave, wide QRS)
Electrophysiology study (definitive, pre-ablation)
Echo (rule out structural heart disease - Ebstein anomaly)
Holter monitor
Exercise stress test (assess for intermittent pre-excitation)
notes:
Delta wave = slurred upstroke of QRS due to ventricular pre-excitation
AV nodal blockers in WPW+AFib can cause 1:1 conduction down accessory pathway → VF
Ebstein anomaly associated with right-sided accessory pathways
Intermittent pre-excitation on Holter suggests lower risk of SCD
Ablation success rate >95%
description: A congenital heart condition where an extra electrical pathway between the upper and lower chambers causes episodes of rapid heartbeat and characteristic ECG changes.

name: Mitral Valve Prolapse
signs:
Mid-systolic click
Late systolic murmur
Often asymptomatic
Palpitations
Atypical chest pain
Anxiety
Dyspnea
Click moves earlier with standing/Valsalva
treatment:
1. Reassurance for asymptomatic patients (benign prognosis)
2. Beta-blockers for symptomatic palpitations
3. Endocarditis prophylaxis NOT recommended (unless prior endocarditis)
4. Surgery if severe mitral regurgitation develops
5. Regular echo follow-up if moderate or greater MR
meds:
Metoprolol 25-50mg BID (palpitations)
Aspirin 81mg daily (if TIA/stroke history)
Anxiolytics if significant anxiety
Endocarditis prophylaxis NOT routinely indicated
ddx: Mitral regurgitation (other causes), Tricuspid valve prolapse, Atrial septal defect, Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, Panic disorder
labs:
Echo (mitral leaflet displacement >=2mm above annular plane)
ECG (usually normal, may show T wave inversions in inferior leads)
Holter monitor if palpitations
Exercise stress test if exertional symptoms
notes:
Most common valvular abnormality (2-3% of population)
Barlow syndrome = myxomatous degeneration with redundant leaflets
Risk of progression to severe MR, endocarditis, arrhythmias
Marfan syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos associated
Click earlier with maneuvers that decrease LV volume (standing, Valsalva)
description: A condition where one or both flaps of the mitral valve bulge back into the left atrium during heartbeat, usually benign but can progress to significant valve leakage.
